Eﬃcient Calculation of Bigram Frequencies in a Corpus of Short Texts

Melvyn Drag, Gauthaman Vasudevan
Avlino, Inc. Holdmel, NJ

Abstract

We show that an e ffi cient and popular method for calculating bigram frequencies is unsuitable for bodies of short texts and o ff er a simple alternative.

d507f61b-5023-4281-b391-f307985b8589.label: text_chunk
d507f61b-5023-4281-b391-f307985b8589.text:
Our method has the same computational complexity as the old method and o ff ers an exact count instead of an approximation.Keywords: NLP, Machine Learning

1. Acknowledgements

This short note is the result of a brief conversation between the authors and Joel Nothman. We came across a potential problem, he gave a sketch of a fix, and we worked out the details of a solution.

2.

a21ef176-da5c-464d-b043-b4614032a257.label: text_chunk
a21ef176-da5c-464d-b043-b4614032a257.text:
Calculating Bigram Frequecies

A common task in natural language processing is to find the most frequently occurring word pairs in a text(s) in the expectation that these pairs will shed some light on the main ideas of the text, or o ff er insight into the structure of the language. One might be interested in pairings of adjacent words, but in some cases one is also interested in pairs of words in some small neighborhood.

41ab694d-c48b-4b70-9faa-d59aa3a2b8c9.text:
Text: “I like kitties and doggies” Window: 4 Bigrams: { (I like), (I kitties), (I and), (like kitties), (like and), (like doggies), (kitties and), (kitties doggies), (and doggies) } .

99e39dc0-2dfa-4377-8f7e-8a7ce0b792ea.text:
The Popular Approximation

Bigram frequencies are often calculated using the approximation
f req( ∗ , word) = f req(word , ∗ ) = f req(word) (1)
In a much cited paper, Church and Hanks [2] use ‘ = ’ in place of ‘ ≈ ’ because the approximation is so good.

8e704f6c-b646-4592-98c9-a36466bee519.label: text_chunk
8e704f6c-b646-4592-98c9-a36466bee519.text:
Indeed, this approximation will only cause errors for the very few words which occur near the beginning or the end of the text.Preprint submitted to arXiv


September 12, 2021
Take for example the text appearing above - the bigram (doggies, *) does not occur once, but the approximation says it does.An e ffi cient method for computing the contingency matrix for a bigram (word1, word2) is suggested by the approximation.

16b78467-e01b-4288-a6a0-89bc0a189b4d.text: Store f req(w1 , w2) for all bigrams (w1 , w2) and the frequencies of all words.

Then,

• f req(word1 , word2) is known,

• f req( ∼ word1 ,

 word2) ≈ f req(word2) - f req(word1 , word2),

• f req(word1 ,

 ∼ word2) ≈ f req(word1) - f req(word1 , word2),

• and f req( ∼ word1 , ∼ word2) is easily computed.

The statistical importance of miscalculations due to this method diminishes as our text grows larger and larger. Interest is growing in the analysis of small texts, however, and a means of computing bigrams for this type of corpus must be employed. This approximation is implemented in popular NLP libraries and can be seen in many tutorials across the internet. People who use this code, or write their own software, must know when it is appropriate.

An Alternative Method

We propose an alternative. As before, store the frequencies of words and the frequencies of bigrams, but this time store two additional maps called too far left and too far right, of the form { word : list of o ff ending indices of word } . The o ff ending indices are those that are either too far to the left or too far to the right for approximation (1) to hold.

3dbeea70-1fe1-40e3-b6dd-9e4744c5136a.label: text_chunk
3dbeea70-1fe1-40e3-b6dd-9e4744c5136a.text: All four of these structures are built during the construction of a bigram finder, and do not cripple performance when computing statistical measures since maps are queried in O(1) time.As an example of the contents of the new maps, in “Dogs are better than cats”, too far left[‘dog’] = [0] for all windows.

In “eight mice eat eight cheese sticks” with window 5, too far left[‘eight’] = [0,3]. For ease of computation the indices stored in too far right are transformed before storage using:
c idx = length − idx − 1 = g(idx) (2)
where length is the length of the small piece of text being analyzed.

Then, too far right[‘cats’] = [g(4) = idx] = [0 = c idx].Now, to compute the exact number of occurrences of a bigram we do the computation:
f req( ∗ , word) = (w − 1) ∗ word f d[word] − N X i = 1 (w − t f l[word][i] − 1) (3)

b080d58f-fbb4-4028-8161-39a1e83fec14.text: where w is the window size being searched for bigrams, w f d is a frequency distribution of all words in the corpus, t f l is the map too far left and N is the number of occurrences of the word in a position too far left.The computation of f req(word , ∗ ) can now be performed in the same way by simply substituting t f l with t f r thanks to transformation g, which reverses the indexing.

The SLRTM (Structured Latent Relational Topic Model) is a sophisticated model designed to enhance the process of generating coherent sentences related to specific topics, known as topic2sentence. It aims to improve user satisfaction by effectively modeling topics of interest to users. The model is built upon the foundational work of Du et al., which discusses the one sentence, one topic assumption, highlighting its relevance in the context of SLRTM.

SLRTM is capable of analyzing words from a dictionary (W) to extract topics from documents and is implemented in C++, showcasing its technical foundation. It has been rigorously tested against baseline models, consistently outperforming them, which underscores its superior capability in modeling document generation processes. The model's performance is evaluated using perplexity, a metric that assesses its effectiveness.

In comparative studies, SLRTM has been shown to outperform other models, including Doc-NADE, in topic modeling tasks. Furthermore, there are plans to integrate SLRTM into RNN-based STC (Sequence-to-Sequence Topic Classification) systems, which would enhance their sensitivity to topics in dialogues. Additionally, SLRTM has a connection to the geographical feature of Crater River, indicating its potential application in analyzing or generating topics related to this area.

1. **Training Data and Labeled Tweets**: Labeled tweets are utilized as training data for sentiment classifiers, which allows these classifiers to learn and enhance their performance in analyzing sentiments.

2. **Impact on Performance**: The quality and characteristics of the training data significantly influence the performance of classifiers. This relationship underscores the critical role that the nature of the training data plays in determining the effectiveness of sentiment analysis models.

3. **Labeled Tweets and Sentiment Classifiers**: Again, it is emphasized that labeled tweets serve as essential training data for sentiment classifiers, reinforcing their ability to improve in sentiment analysis tasks.

Overall, the summary illustrates that labeled tweets are fundamental to the training process of sentiment classifiers, and the quality of this training data is pivotal in shaping the classifiers' performance outcomes.
